result.markdown:
--- title: "GIS with R - Download OpenStreetMap data with osmdata package" date: "2022-11-13" categories: [R, GIS] image: gis_thumbnail.jpg format: html: code-tools: true df-print: paged fontsize: 0.9em editor: visual --- ::: {style="text-align: justify"} The `osmdata` package can be used to download OpenStreetMap data. [OpenStreetMap](https://www.openstreetmap.org/#map=8/43.333/13.052) is a database of crowdsourced geographic information that includes things like road networks, water features, points of interest and more. Whatever a person on the internet can map and has mapped on OSM is available for download for free. For a more detailed introduction with more exploration of the package functionalities, see the [vignette](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/osmdata/vignettes/osmdata.html#22_Extracting_OSM_data_from_a_query). ## Installing osmdata You can install the most recent official version of `osmdata` from CRAN with: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} install.packages("osmdata") ``` ::: Or the developer version from GitHub: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} devtools::install_github("ropensci/osmdata") ``` ::: We start by loading the needed packages. In the following sections I'll provide many examples on how to use `osmdata` to query spatial data from OSM database. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(osmdata) library(ggplot2) ``` ::: ## Get bounding boxes `osmdata::getbb()` function uses the free [Nominatim API](https://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/Nominatim) provided by OpenStreetMap to find the bounding box (bb) associated with place names. For example: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} italy_bb <- osmdata::getbb("Italy", featuretype = "Country") brazil_bb <- osmdata::getbb("Brazil", featuretype = "Country") list(italy = italy_bb, brazil = brazil_bb) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` $italy min max x 6.627266 18.78447 y 35.288962 47.09215 $brazil min max x -73.98306 -28.628965 y -33.86891 5.269581 ``` ::: ::: Note that I set the `featuretype` parameter to 'Country'. This ensures the correct bounding boxes for the actual Italy and Brazil countries (and not entities with similar/same name) are retrieved. The default value of `featuretype` is, in fact, "settlement", that combines results from all intermediate levels below "country" and and above "streets". See `?osmdata::getbb()` for more details, and '[Search \> Parameters' section](https://nominatim.org/release-docs/develop/api/Search/) from Nominatim API documentation for a list of available `featuretype` values. For example, let's try without adding `featuretype` parameter : ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} italy_no_ft <- osmdata::getbb("Italy", format_out = "data.frame") italy_no_ft ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
````` ::: ::: As you can see, there are many non-country entities that match the name 'Italy', like 'Italy, Ellis County, Texas, 76651, United States', or 'Italy, Georgetown County, South Carolina, 29510, United States'. So, running `osmdata::getbb("Italy")` without `featuretype = "Country"` would return the wrong output. ::: callout-note ## Note: `format_out` parameter lets the user choose the output format. Default format is 'matrix'. See `?osmdata::getbb()` for a list of available formats. ::: ## Overpass queries `overpass` is a C++ library that serves OpenStreetMap data over the web. All `overpass` queries with `osmdata` package begin with the function `osmdata::opq()` . The function requires a bounding box as input (see `?osmdata::opq()` for more details). ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} italy <- osmdata::opq(bbox = italy_bb) italy ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` $bbox [1] "35.2889616,6.6272658,47.0921462,18.7844746" $prefix [1] "[out:xml][timeout:25];\n(\n" $suffix [1] ");\n(._;>;);\nout body;" $features NULL attr(,"class") [1] "list" "overpass_query" attr(,"nodes_only") [1] FALSE ``` ::: ::: Increase `timeout` and/or `memsize` parameters values if you get timeout and/or memory errors. We can also filter the base, more general, query by adding **OSM** **features**, which are specified in terms of key-value pairs. We do this with `osmdata::add_osm_feature()` function. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} italy <- osmdata::opq(bbox = italy_bb, timeout = 50000) |> add_osm_feature(key = "admin_level", value = 4) italy ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` $bbox [1] "35.2889616,6.6272658,47.0921462,18.7844746" $prefix [1] "[out:xml][timeout:50000];\n(\n" $suffix [1] ");\n(._;>;);\nout body;" $features [1] " [\"admin_level\"=\"4\"]" attr(,"class") [1] "list" "overpass_query" attr(,"nodes_only") [1] FALSE ``` ::: ::: **`admin_level`** key describes the administrative level of a feature within a government hierarchy. It is primarily used for the borders of territorial political entities (e.g. country, state, municipality) together with `boundary = administrative`. Due to cultural and political differences, admin levels of different countries only correspond approximately to each other. Here, `value = 4` stands for boundaries of Italian regions. For a list of all the available key-value pairs, see [Map features page](https://wiki.openstreetmap.org/wiki/Map_features#Boundary) on openstreetmap website. You can also run `osmdata::available_features()` and `osmdata::available_tags()` functions. The first one returns a vector including all the available keys. The second one returns a vector including all the available values for a specific key. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} keys <- data.frame(key = osmdata::available_features()) vals <- osmdata::available_tags("boundary") ``` ::: ::: {.cell rows.print='5'} ```{.r .cell-code} keys ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
````` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} vals ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "aboriginal_lands" "administrative" "border_zone" [4] "disputed" "forest" "forest_compartment" [7] "hazard" "maritime" "marker" [10] "national_park" "place" "political" [13] "postal_code" "protected_area" "special_economic_zone" ``` ::: ::: ## Extracting OSM data from an overpass query After defining a query, we download the actual data by adding a `osmdata::osmdata_sf()` or `osmdata::osmdata_sp()` call, to return the data in `sf` or `sp` format. In the following example we download the boundary of the provinces of Marche region, in Italy. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} prov <- c("Ancona", "Ascoli Piceno", "Fermo", "Macerata", "Pesaro e Urbino") marche <- osmdata::opq(bbox = getbb("Marche, Italy", featuretype = "state"), timeout = 50000) |> add_osm_feature(key = "admin_level", value = 6) |> add_osm_feature(key = "name", value = prov) |> osmdata_sf() marche ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Object of class 'osmdata' with: $bbox : 42.6871561,12.1854536,43.9715999,13.9163435 $overpass_call : The call submitted to the overpass API $meta : metadata including timestamp and version numbers $osm_points : 'sf' Simple Features Collection with 25472 points $osm_lines : 'sf' Simple Features Collection with 303 linestrings $osm_polygons : 'sf' Simple Features Collection with 2 polygons $osm_multilines : NULL $osm_multipolygons : 'sf' Simple Features Collection with 5 multipolygons ``` ::: ::: `admin_level=6` stands for provinces, and with `name=prov` we filter only the actual Marche provinces, since the rectangular bounding box defined with `bbox` argument also encompasses parts of other italian regions. As you can see, we got an `osmdata` object with many different features as output, including 5 multipolygons. Those are the polygons representing the provinces. First let's see if those multipolygons have a defined CRS attribute (see [GIS with R - Coordinate Reference Systems in R](https://g-bez.github.io/my-portfolio/posts/gis_crs/) post for more details about coordinate reference systems). ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sf::st_crs(marche$osm_multipolygons)$input ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "EPSG:4326" ``` ::: ::: The CRS is set to WGS84. Let's change it to a more appropriate value. Marche region falls into UTM zone 33N, so let's find the corresponding `EPSG` code with the following chunk. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} crs <- rgdal::make_EPSG() marche_crs <- crs[crs$note == "WGS 72 / UTM zone 33N", "code"] marche_crs ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 32233 ``` ::: ::: Then we run `sf::st_transform()` function to transform our data into the new CRS. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} marche$osm_multipolygons <- sf::st_transform(marche$osm_multipolygons, crs = marche_crs) sf::st_crs(marche$osm_multipolygons)$input ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "EPSG:32233" ``` ::: ::: Finally I provide a quick `ggplot2` plot of Marche provinces. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} p <- ggplot2::ggplot() + geom_sf(data = marche$osm_multipolygons, aes(geometry = geometry, fill = name), colour = "black", alpha = .23) + theme(panel.grid = element_blank(), legend.position = "bottom", legend.title = element_blank(), legend.text = element_text(size = 8), legend.key.size = unit(3, "mm")) + labs(title = "Marche provinces", xlab = "lon", ylab = "lat") p ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-14-1.png){width=100% height=250%} ::: ::: Now let's say we want to refine our `osmdata` query, and also download and visualize data about the primary and secondary highways in Marche region, on top of the map we just drawn. First we start by executing a new separate query. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} bb_poly <- osmdata::getbb("Marche, Italy", featuretype = "state", format_out = "polygon") highways <- osmdata::opq(bbox = getbb("Marche, Italy", featuretype = "state"), timeout = 50000) |> add_osm_feature(key = "highway", value = c("primary", "secondary")) |> osmdata_sf() |> trim_osmdata(bb_poly) |> unique_osmdata() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` bb_poly has more than one polygon; the first will be selected. ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} highways ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Object of class 'osmdata' with: $bbox : 42.6871561,12.1854536,43.9715999,13.9163435 $overpass_call : The call submitted to the overpass API $meta : metadata including timestamp and version numbers $osm_points : 'sf' Simple Features Collection with 1033 points $osm_lines : 'sf' Simple Features Collection with 11111 linestrings $osm_polygons : 'sf' Simple Features Collection with 440 polygons $osm_multilines : NULL $osm_multipolygons : NULL ``` ::: ::: ::: callout-note ## Note: In this case, filtering the result by the names of Marche highways is not a good solution, since there are thousands of roads. So we do a polygon filtering by defining a polygon bounding box with `format_out = "polygon"` and then trimming the query result with `osmdata::trim_osmdata()` function. I also cleaned the output from possible duplicates with `osmdata::unique_osmdata()` function. ::: Here the features of interest are the lines ('osm_lines'). Again, we convert the data into the appropriate CRS. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} highways$osm_lines <- sf::st_transform(highways$osm_lines, crs = marche_crs) sf::st_crs(highways$osm_lines)$input ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "EPSG:32233" ``` ::: ::: In the end, we add the highways layer to the previous plot. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} p_highways <- p + geom_sf(data = highways$osm_lines, aes(geometry = geometry, colour = name), show.legend = F) p_highways ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-17-1.png){width=100% height=250%} ::: ::: :::
